output.explanation: Case/convention inconsistency: most values are lowercase language codes (e.g., "eng"), while this is uppercase "ENG".
Unexpected language-code length: most codes are 2–3 lowercase letters, but this one ("pol") does not match the established set seen elsewhere (e.g., "por", "spa", "fre", "eng", "kor", "chi", "jpn").

Malformed multi-language entry: combines full words and a semicolon pattern ("English; ABSTRACT LANGUAGE:English") unlike other simple labels seen (e.g., "English", or whitespace-separated codes like "eng     por").
Unexpected code length/pattern vs. dominant set: most 3-letter codes correspond to established languages/labels in the list; "kor" is not aligned with the only Korean label present as "Korean".
Unexpected code vs. full label: Chinese is represented as "Chinese     ..." and "English" in other entries; single "chi" deviates from those established representations.

Concatenated multi-language code: "engspa" appears as a merged token, while other multi-language code combinations are separated by spaces (e.g., none shown as concatenation; "ENGPOR" is uppercase but also merged).
Contains multiple languages separated by excessive whitespace in a single value ("eng     por"), unlike the consistent single-language codes/words elsewhere.
Extra-long mixed entry: includes multiple languages separated by large whitespace within a single value ("Chinese     Chinese, English"), unlike the simpler single-language labels/codes.
Multi-language mixed formatting with large whitespace ("Chinese     English, Chinese") does not match the dominant simple single-word/code pattern.
Rare/isolated code not matching dominant established labels/codes in the list ("dut" only appears once; others use common patterns like "ger", "fre", "ita").
Isolated 3-letter code that does not match the established set of language labels/codes shown ("cze" only appears once; similar to other unused code forms).
Proper noun/case mismatch vs. dominant code style: other languages are given as lowercase codes (e.g., "eng", "ger", "spa"), while this is capitalized full word.
Merged multi-language uppercase token: "ENGPOR" is concatenated (no separator) and uppercase, while the list otherwise uses lowercase codes and/or separated multi-language formats.

Single 3-letter code not in the dominant set besides similar ones; here "por" is present elsewhere, but this value alone is too rare/inconsistent compared to full-word "German/Chinese"? (Syntactically, still a code.)
Rare isolated code not matching the common set seen ("tur" only once; others have counterparts like "English", "German", etc.).
Concatenated multi-language code without separator ("engfre"), unlike other multi-language representations that are separated.
Merged lowercase multi-language code without separator ("engpor"), unlike the space-separated multi-language example "eng     por" and single-language patterns.
Rare isolated code ("heb" only once) not aligned with the dominant small set of frequently recurring codes.
Case mismatch: "Eng" (capital E) vs dominant lowercase code "eng".
Full language name not consistent with nearby code-style entries (most entries are 2–3 letter codes like "en", "pt", "fre").

Too-short code compared to most 2–3 letter codes shown; only other 2-letter code is "en" with count 1, but this one is exactly "en"—however it’s inconsistent as a lowercase code within the established mix of single-word "English" and codes. Keep consistent with "English" representation.
Multi-language full-word entry with large whitespace ("English     Italian") deviates from single-language "English" or code-based patterns.
Rare isolated 3-letter code ("rus" only once) not consistent with the more common code/word patterns observed for other languages.
Isolated 2-letter code not matching the dominant 3-letter lowercase code set used for most languages (e.g., "eng", "fre", "spa", "ger").
